question: [File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user probe transaction existence by comparing NotFoundError (404) vs InvalidArgumentError (400) responses to enumerate valid transaction IDs and reconstruct a partial transaction ordering without any authentication, causing a reorganized view of transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that shouldReturnMessage() returns the raw err.message for all 4xx errors to extract transaction-specific details (e.g., timestamp ranges, sequence numbers) from InvalidArgumentError messages, enabling reconstruction of transaction ordering?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger an error path where the m.data field is included in the JSON response, leaking internal transaction data objects that reveal transaction ordering or history metadata?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user use the elapsed time value (Date.now() - startTime) embedded in server logs—observable indirectly via response timing—to infer whether a queried transaction timestamp exists in the database, enabling timeline reconstruction of transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: shouldReturnMessage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that shouldReturnMessage() returns true for httpStatusCodes.BAD_GATEWAY (502) to receive raw upstream error messages that contain transaction routing or ordering metadata from backend services?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger an error where errorMessages is an array containing objects with both message and detail fields, causing the response to expose structured transaction metadata (e.g., conflicting timestamps) that reveals history reorganization?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: handleRejection()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger an unhandled promise rejection via handleRejection() that only logs but does not abort the in-flight request, allowing a partially-processed transaction history response to be served to the client with an incomplete or reordered set of records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user submit a request with a crafted timestamp parameter that triggers a RangeError (mapped to 400 BAD_REQUEST), and use the raw err.message returned by shouldReturnMessage() to learn the valid timestamp boundary of the transaction history, enabling targeted history probing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: errorMessageFormat()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger an error where errorMessages contains an object with a data property holding an array of transaction records, causing errorMessageFormat() to include those records in the _status.messages response and exposing unfiltered transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/middleware/httpErrorHandler.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: handleError()] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent requests that each trigger handleError() with overlapping res.locals[requestStartTime] values, causing elapsed time calculations to produce misleading timing data that obscures the true ordering of transaction processing?
